title: Spaghetti al Pomodoro
subtitle: San Marzano tomatoes, basil, and olive oil — the most important Italian sauce
description: Spaghetti al pomodoro is the foundation of Italian cooking: a short-cooked, bright tomato sauce made from the best tinned tomatoes you can buy, a handful of basil, and generous extra-virgin olive oil. The sauce is deliberately not cooked for long — twenty minutes on the hob is enough to concentrate the flavour while keeping the tomatoes fresh and slightly acidic. The better the tomato, the better the dish.
sourceNotes: The simplest expression of the Neapolitan tomato sauce tradition. Pellegrino Artusi's La Scienza in Cucina (1891) contains an early version; Ada Boni's Il Talismano della Felicità (1927) codifies the short-cook method that remains standard today.

recipeTools.notes: For the pasta — at least 3 litres of water to cook spaghetti properly.
For the sauce — a straight-sided pan allows even reduction.
For transferring spaghetti to the sauce pan.

glossaryTerms.definition: A variety of plum tomato grown in the volcanic soil near Naples, prized for their low acidity, sweet flavour, and meaty flesh. The tinned version is the standard ingredient for Italian tomato sauces.
The starchy, salted water pasta cooks in. A splash added to the sauce helps it cling to the pasta strands.

ingredients.notes: San Marzano if possible — crush them by hand before adding
Half for cooking, half to finish
Leaves only — a few added during cooking, the rest to finish

steps: Crush the tinned tomatoes by hand directly over the pan, squeezing each one flat. Discard any tough core pieces.
Warm half the olive oil in a medium saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the crushed garlic cloves and cook for 2 minutes, turning once, until fragrant but not browned.
Add the crushed tomatoes with all their juice and a few basil leaves. Season with salt. Stir and bring to a gentle simmer.
Cook uncovered for 18–20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce has thickened slightly and the colour has deepened. Remove and discard the garlic cloves.
Meanwhile, cook the spaghetti in generously salted boiling water until 1 minute short of the packet time. Reserve a ladle of pasta water before draining.
Transfer the spaghetti to the sauce with tongs, adding a splash of pasta water. Toss over a medium-low heat for 1 minute until the sauce clings to each strand.
Remove from the heat. Add the remaining olive oil and the fresh basil leaves. Toss once more and serve immediately.

content.text: With four ingredients in the sauce, quality is everything. Use the best tinned
tomatoes you can find and an olive oil worth tasting on its own. Crushing the tomatoes by hand rather than using a blender leaves uneven pieces that give the sauce texture.

Crush {{tinned-whole-plum-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes by hand directly over the pan — squeeze each one flat, discarding any tough core. Warm half of {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a medium saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add 2 lightly crushed garlic cloves and cook for 2 minutes until fragrant but not coloured. Add the crushed tomatoes with all their juice, a few basil leaves, and salt. Bring to a gentle simmer.
Cook uncovered for 18–20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce has thickened and the colour has deepened. Remove the garlic cloves and discard.
Cook {{spaghetti}} of spaghetti in generously salted water until 1 minute short of the packet time. Reserve a ladle of

. Transfer the spaghetti to the sauce with tongs, add a splash of pasta water, and toss over a medium-low heat for 1 minute. Remove from the heat, add the remaining olive oil and the fresh basil, toss once more, and serve immediately.

Long-cooked tomato sauces go darker and sweeter as the sugars caramelise. The short cook here keeps the tomatoes bright and slightly acidic — closer to a fresh tomato flavour. That freshness is the point. If the sauce tastes too sharp, a small pinch of sugar brings it back; if it tastes flat, it needs more salt rather than more cooking.
